title: What Is LMArena? Crowdsourced Model Rankings
seoTitle: What Is LMArena? Blind A/B Elo Model Leaderboard
metaDescription: LMArena ranks models by crowdsourced blind A/B votes turned into Elo ratings based on human preference. Learn how the leaderboard works and its limits.
oneLiner: You will understand how LMArena converts blind human preference votes into an Elo leaderboard and where that signal falls short.

blocks.md: Most AI benchmarks work like a school exam: a model answers a fixed set of questions and gets a score for how many it got right. That tells you something, but not everything. A model can ace the test and still feel clumsy, robotic, or unhelpful when a real person talks to it. **LMArena** measures the thing exams miss — which model people actually *prefer* to use.

Here is the whole idea. You type a question into LMArena. It sends that same question to two anonymous models — you don't know which ones. Both replies appear side by side. You read them and vote for the better one. That's it. Do this across hundreds of thousands of votes from real users, and a ranking falls out: a leaderboard of models sorted by how often humans pick them.

Think of a blind taste test for soft drinks. Two unlabeled cups, you sip both, you point at the one you like more. Nobody is reading a nutrition label or a lab report — they're just tasting. Run that test thousands of times with thousands of people and you learn which drink wins on raw preference. LMArena is that taste test, but for AI chat models, and the 'taste' is the quality of the answer.

The project started at UC Berkeley as **Chatbot Arena**, run by a group called **LMSYS**. It was later renamed **LMArena** and spun out into its own organization. You'll still see all three names — *LMSYS*, *Chatbot Arena*, *LMArena* — used interchangeably for the same blind-vote leaderboard.

Static benchmarks like [MMLU](/learn/evaluation-safety/benchmarks-leaderboards/what-is-mmlu-benchmark) have a built-in weakness: the questions are public and fixed. Once a test exists on the internet, its answers can leak into the next model's training data — that's [benchmark contamination](/learn/evaluation-safety/benchmarks-leaderboards/benchmark-contamination) — and labs can quietly tune their models to score well on it. A high score can mean 'genuinely smart' or just 'practiced this exact test.' You can't always tell.

LMArena sidesteps a lot of that. The prompts come from real users in the moment, so there's no fixed question set to memorize ahead of time. And it measures something fixed exams can't: *how good does this feel to a human in open-ended conversation?* That covers tone, helpfulness, clarity, formatting, and whether the answer actually solves the problem the way a person wanted — qualities no multiple-choice test captures.

items: **It reflects real preference, not just correctness.** Two answers can both be factually right while one is far more useful. Humans reward the useful one.
**It's hard to game with memorization.** There's no answer key to study. To climb the board, a model has to be broadly good across whatever real users throw at it.
**It compares everything on one scale.** Open and closed models, big and small, from any lab — all ranked head-to-head by the same blind-vote method.
**It's a fast public signal.** When a new model lands, its Arena position is one of the first widely-cited read-outs on whether people like it.

blocks.md: For a builder choosing a model, the Arena ranking is a useful *starting* filter — it tells you what a broad crowd prefers in general chat. It does not tell you what's best for *your* task, which is why you should still run [your own evals](/learn/evaluation-safety/benchmarks-leaderboards/public-vs-private-benchmarks). More on that limit below.

Under the hood, LMArena turns a pile of one-on-one votes into a single number per model. The method is borrowed from chess: the **Elo rating system**. Each model has a rating; when one model 'beats' another in a vote, the winner's rating goes up and the loser's goes down. Beat a much higher-rated model and you gain a lot; beat a weaker one and you gain only a little. Over many matchups, ratings settle into a stable ranking.

diagram.title: From one vote to a leaderboard

blocks.md: The names are hidden *on purpose*, and only revealed after you vote. If you knew one answer came from a famous lab and the other from an unknown one, you might favor the brand instead of the actual answer. Blinding removes that bias, so the vote reflects the *response*, not the reputation. It's the same reason a taste test peels the labels off the cups.

blocks.text: Why Elo, not a simple win percentage

blocks.md: A plain win rate would be unfair, because not every model faces the same opponents. A model that mostly battles weak ones would look great on raw wins. Elo fixes this by weighting *who* you beat: the rating estimates true strength by accounting for the strength of each opponent. Modern Arena leaderboards use a statistical refinement of classic Elo (a method based on the **Bradley–Terry model**) that also reports confidence intervals — a sense of how *sure* the ranking is, not just the point estimate.

An Elo number is only meaningful *relative to other models on the same board*, and the values drift as new models and votes arrive. Never memorize an absolute score — read the *order* and the gaps, and check whether two models' confidence intervals overlap before calling one 'better.'

LMArena also publishes **category leaderboards** — for example coding, math, longer conversations, or instruction-following — because a model that wins on casual chat may not win on hard reasoning. Looking at the category that matches your use case is far more informative than the single overall number.

sections.title: A walk through a single battle

blocks.md: Concretely, here's what one round looks like from your seat. Say you type: *"Explain why the sky is blue to a 10-year-old."*

items: LMArena secretly picks two models — call them **Model A** and **Model B**. You see no names.
Both get your exact prompt and reply. You read both answers in two columns.
You decide. Maybe A is accurate but dry, while B is accurate *and* uses a fun analogy a kid would get. You click **B is better**. (You can also pick *tie* or *both are bad*.)
Only now are the names revealed. Your single vote becomes one matchup result feeding the Elo math.

blocks.md: One vote barely moves anything — it's noisy. The signal emerges from *aggregation*. When hundreds of thousands of people each run their own battles on their own prompts, the random noise averages out and a genuine preference ranking remains. This is the core strength and the core caveat of the method, captured below.

diagram.title: What a vote does and doesn't tell you

columns.title: One vote
items: Reflects one person, one prompt
Subjective and noisy
Easy to flip on a coin-toss tie
Means almost nothing alone

columns.title: Hundreds of thousands of votes
items: Noise averages out
Stable preference signal
Confidence intervals tighten
A trustworthy ranking emerges

blocks.md: LMArena and fixed-question benchmarks answer different questions, so the smart move is to read them together, not to pick one. A model can top the Arena on likability while a coding benchmark like [SWE-bench](/learn/evaluation-safety/benchmarks-leaderboards/what-is-swe-bench) shows it's weaker at real bug-fixing — both facts can be true at once.

rows: No answer key — it's a judgment
Hard to memorize; can be style-gamed

blocks.md: Rule of thumb: use the Arena to see what a broad crowd prefers overall, use a task benchmark to check a specific skill, and use [your own private eval set](/learn/evaluation-safety/benchmarks-leaderboards/public-vs-private-benchmarks) to decide what's actually best for *your* product. No public number replaces testing on your real data.
The Arena is valuable but not the last word. Knowing its blind spots keeps you from over-trusting a leaderboard position.

items: **Style and length bias.** Humans tend to reward answers that *look* good — longer, nicely formatted, confident, lots of bullet points and emoji — even when a shorter answer is just as correct. A model can climb partly by being agreeable and verbose rather than more accurate. Arena maintainers publish 'style-controlled' rankings to dampen this, but it never fully disappears.

**It measures preference, not truth.** Voters often can't verify whether an answer is factually correct. A confident, well-written wrong answer can beat a hedged right one. The Arena rewards *persuasiveness* as much as *correctness*.

**Prompt mix skews toward casual use.** Real users ask a lot of everyday questions and relatively few extreme edge cases, so the overall score leans toward general chat quality. Check category boards for harder skills.

**Votes are subjective and self-selected.** The people who use the Arena aren't a controlled sample of all users, and tastes differ. The crowd's preference is a real signal, not an objective ground truth.
**Ratings move.** A number that's true this month can shift as new models and votes arrive. Treat any specific value as a snapshot, not a constant.

blocks.md: Don't pick a production model on Arena rank alone. A high position means 'a broad crowd liked its general answers,' which can hide weaknesses on *your* specific task — accuracy on your data, latency, cost, safety behavior, and tool use. Always confirm with your own evaluation before you ship.
Once the basic taste-test picture clicks, a few finer points are worth understanding before you lean on Arena numbers in a decision.

**The Bradley–Terry refinement.** Classic chess Elo updates ratings sequentially, vote by vote, which makes the final numbers depend a little on the *order* the games happened. Modern Arena leaderboards instead fit all the votes at once with a statistical model (Bradley–Terry, the math behind pairwise-comparison ranking) and report **confidence intervals**. The practical takeaway: when two models' intervals overlap, they're effectively tied — the ranking can't tell them apart yet, no matter what the point estimates say. For a deeper treatment of the rating math, see [how Arena Elo ratings work](/learn/evaluation-safety/benchmarks-leaderboards/lmarena-elo-ratings-explained).

**Style control.** Because length and formatting sway voters, the maintainers fit a version of the model that statistically *adjusts out* the effect of answer length and markdown. The style-controlled board is closer to 'which answer is better, holding presentation equal.' If you care about substance over polish, that's the column to read.

**Overfitting to the crowd.** Once the Arena becomes a target labs care about, there's pressure to optimize for whatever wins votes — friendliness, eagerness, a chatty tone — which isn't always what makes a model genuinely more capable. This is a soft cousin of [benchmark overfitting](/learn/evaluation-safety/benchmarks-leaderboards/benchmark-overfitting): the metric subtly reshapes the thing it measures. It's a reason to watch *multiple* signals, not crown one leaderboard.

**Where it fits in the bigger picture.** LMArena is one instrument in a panel. Pair it with task benchmarks for specific skills, an aggregator view of cost and speed, and your own offline tests. To zoom out on the whole landscape — what benchmarks are, what they can and can't show, and how to read a score without being fooled — start with [what LLM benchmarks are](/learn/evaluation-safety/benchmarks-leaderboards/what-are-llm-benchmarks) and [how to read a benchmark score](/learn/evaluation-safety/benchmarks-leaderboards/how-to-read-a-benchmark-score). The recurring lesson: a leaderboard rank is a useful clue, never a verdict.

faq.a: LMArena is a crowdsourced leaderboard that ranks AI chat models by human preference. Users type their own prompts, get two anonymous responses side by side, and vote for the better one. Aggregated over many votes, those blind comparisons produce an Elo-style ranking. It started at UC Berkeley as Chatbot Arena (run by LMSYS) and was later renamed LMArena.

faq.q: How does LMArena calculate its rankings?
faq.a: Each blind A/B vote is a head-to-head matchup. LMArena feeds these into a rating system based on Elo (refined with the Bradley–Terry statistical model) where beating a stronger model raises your rating more than beating a weaker one. Modern boards fit all votes at once and report confidence intervals, so you can see how certain a ranking is.

faq.q: Why are the model names hidden during voting?
faq.a: To remove brand bias. If you knew one answer came from a famous lab, you might favor it regardless of quality. Hiding the names until after you vote means your choice reflects the actual response, not its reputation — the same reason a taste test peels labels off the cups.

faq.q: Is LMArena the same as LMSYS and Chatbot Arena?
faq.a: Effectively yes — they're names for the same blind-vote leaderboard at different points in its history. It began as Chatbot Arena under the LMSYS group at UC Berkeley, then was renamed LMArena and spun out as its own organization. You'll see all three names used for the same project.

faq.q: What are the main limitations of the LMArena leaderboard?
faq.a: It measures preference, not truth — a confident, well-formatted wrong answer can beat a correct one. Voters favor longer, prettier responses (style bias), the prompt mix leans casual, and ratings drift as new votes arrive. Treat the rank as one signal and confirm with your own evaluation before choosing a production model.

faq.q: Should I pick a model based on its LMArena rank?
faq.a: Use it as a starting filter, not the final answer. A high Arena rank means a broad crowd liked the model's general chat, which can hide weaknesses on your specific task, plus cost, latency, and safety behavior. Check the relevant category board and always run your own eval on your real data before shipping.
